questions.question: Which of the following features is typical of arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy, associated with giant cell arteritis, presents with elevated ESR (>50 mm/h), unlike the non-arteritic form where ESR is usually normal.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: The treatment of choice is high-dose systemic corticosteroids to prevent visual loss in the other eye and control vascular inflammation in giant cell arteritis.

questions.question: Which temporal artery biopsy finding confirms the diagnosis of arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy shows granulomas with multinucleated giant cells in the arterial wall, characteristic of the giant cell arteritis that causes the arteritic form.

questions.question: What is the main risk factor for developing non-arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: High blood pressure is a major risk factor for the non-arteritic form, along with diabetes and other vasculopathies, unlike the arteritic form, which is associated with vasculitis.

questions.question: Which systemic symptom is characteristic of arteritic but not nonarteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Mandibular claudication is a classic symptom of giant cell arteritis, present in the arteritic form but absent in the non-arteritic form, which is purely ocular.

questions.question: What is the characteristic fundus finding of nonarteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In the non-arteritic form, disc edema is typically diffuse and pale, while in the arteritic form it can be more intense and associated with retinal necrosis.

questions.question: What is the main cause of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy in the elderly?
questions.explanation: Giant cell arteritis is the leading cause of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy in patients over 50 years of age, due to inflammation of the posterior ciliary arteries.

questions.question: What systemic symptom is characteristic in patients with ischemic optic neuropathy due to giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: Mandibular claudication is a classic symptom of giant cell arteritis, which, together with ischemic optic neuropathy, guides the diagnosis of this vasculitis.

questions.question: Which laboratory test is typically elevated in ischemic optic neuropathy associated with giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is markedly elevated in giant cell arteritis, being a key finding in the diagnosis of this ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for ischemic optic neuropathy due to giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids in high doses (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day) are the first-line treatment to prevent bilateral visual loss in this ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: Which visual complication is most feared in ischemic optic neuropathy due to untreated giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: Without prompt treatment, giant cell arteritis can cause bilateral and irreversible ischemic optic neuropathy, leading to permanent total blindness.

questions.question: Which temporal artery biopsy finding confirms the association with giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: The presence of multinucleated giant cells in the arterial wall, together with inflammatory infiltrate, is the characteristic histopathological finding of giant cell arteritis.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy is caused by hypoperfusion of the optic nerve, generally associated with microangiopathy in patients with vascular risk factors such as hypertension and diabetes.

questions.question: Which field finding is typical in anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: The lower altitudinal defect is characteristic of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, reflecting damage to the optic nerve fibers that supply the upper sector of the retina.

questions.question: What is the main modifiable risk factor for non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is the main modifiable risk factor, since diabetic microangiopathy compromises the irrigation of the optic nerve, predisposing to ischemia.

questions.question: What imaging test is ordered to rule out giant cell arteritis in suspected ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Fluorescein angiography can show delayed choroidal filling in giant cell arteritis, helping to differentiate the arteritic from the non-arteritic form.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Intravenous corticosteroids are the first-line treatment in arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, since the underlying cause is an inflammatory vasculitis that requires urgent immune suppression.

questions.question: What clinical feature differentiates anterior ischemic optic neuropathy from optic neuritis?
questions.explanation: Pain with eye movements is characteristic of optic neuritis, while anterior ischemic optic neuropathy is usually painless, which helps in differential diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the characteristic clinical finding of relative afferent pupillary defect?
questions.explanation: The relative afferent pupillary defect manifests as a paradoxical dilation of both pupils when the affected eye is illuminated, due to a damaged afferent pathway that reduces the consensual response.

questions.question: What anatomical structure is primarily affected in the relative afferent pupillary defect?
questions.explanation: The relative afferent pupillary defect is caused by a lesion in the afferent pathway of the pupillary reflex, mainly the optic nerve, which prevents the adequate transmission of the light stimulus.

questions.question: Which of the following pathologies is a common cause of relative afferent pupillary defect?
questions.explanation: Optic neuritis, due to inflammation of the optic nerve, affects the afferent pathway of the pupillary reflex, being a classic cause of relative afferent pupillary defect in clinical examination.

questions.question: What maneuver is used to detect relative afferent pupillary defect?
questions.explanation: The swinging flashlight test consists of illuminating both eyes alternately; In the relative afferent pupillary defect, the pupil of the affected eye paradoxically dilates when receiving light.

questions.question: What other sign is classically associated with the relative afferent pupillary defect in ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, the relative afferent pupillary defect is typically accompanied by an altitudinal scotoma, which is a visual loss in the upper or lower field.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for relative afferent pupillary defect caused by optic neuritis?
questions.explanation: In optic neuritis, treatment with intravenous corticosteroids accelerates visual recovery and reduces inflammation of the optic nerve, although the relative afferent pupillary defect may persist.

questions.question: What is the characteristic funduscopic finding in acute anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In the acute phase of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION), the typical funduscopic finding is optic disc edema, which can be diffuse or sectoral, associated with peripapillary hemorrhages.

questions.question: Which artery is most frequently involved in anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) is caused by hypoperfusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries, which supply the head of the optic nerve, especially in its prelaminar and laminar portion.

questions.question: What is the most relevant risk factor for developing non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal hypotension, especially in patients with hypertension or diabetes, reduces perfusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries during sleep, triggering optic disc ischemia in nonarteritic AION.

questions.question: What imaging study is used to confirm optic disc edema in the acute phase?
questions.explanation: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) allows quantifying the thickness of the peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer, confirming optic disc edema in the acute phase of AION with high sensitivity.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In arteritic AION, associated with giant cell arteritis, the treatment of choice is high-dose systemic corticosteroids (methylprednisolone or prednisone) to prevent visual loss in the contralateral eye and systemic complications.

questions.question: What systemic clinical sign may accompany arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Mandibular claudication is a classic symptom of giant cell arteritis, which causes arteritic AION. It occurs due to ischemia of the masticatory muscles when chewing, and its presence guides the diagnosis of the arteritic variant.

questions.question: What field defect is characteristic of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: The lower altitude defect is the most classic field finding in anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, due to the selective involvement of the nerve fibers of the upper sector of the optic nerve.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is the most common risk factor associated with the non-arteritic form, due to microangiopathy that compromises the blood supply to the optic nerve head.

questions.question: What imaging study is used to confirm the diagnosis of arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is the gold standard to confirm giant cell arteritis, the cause of the arteritic form of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, showing gigantocellular inflammatory infiltrate.

questions.question: Which fundus finding is typical in the acute phase of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Optic disc edema is the cardinal fundoscopic sign in the acute phase, resulting from infarction of the prelaminar sector of the optic nerve, which causes bulging and effacement of the papillary edges.

questions.question: What treatment has been shown to improve visual recovery in non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: To date, no treatment has demonstrated proven efficacy to improve visual acuity or visual field in the nonarteritic form; Management focuses on controlling cardiovascular risk factors.

questions.question: What differentiates arteritic from non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy on campimetry?
options: More severe and rapid vision loss
questions.explanation: In the arteritic form, visual loss is usually more severe and has a more rapid onset, often leading to non-perception of light, due to inflammatory occlusion of the short posterior ciliary arteries.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic of arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy, associated with giant cell arteritis, ESR and CRP are usually elevated as markers of systemic inflammation.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids are the initial treatment of choice to prevent contralateral visual loss in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: Which biopsy confirms the diagnosis of arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy shows granulomatous inflammation with giant cells, confirming giant cell arteritis causing arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: What is the most feared complication of untreated arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Without timely corticosteroid treatment, arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy can progress to irreversible bilateral visual loss due to involvement of the second eye.

questions.question: What classic systemic symptom accompanies arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Temporal headache and jaw claudication are classic symptoms of giant cell arteritis, associated with arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: What imaging test can support the diagnosis of arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound of temporal arteries may show the perivascular hypoechoic halo sign, indicative of giant cell arteritis in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy, associated with giant cell arteritis, requires intravenous corticosteroids in high doses as an emergency to prevent irreversible visual loss.

questions.question: Which artery is typically affected in arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy is due to inflammation of the ophthalmic artery or its branches, secondary to giant cell arteritis, compromising the blood supply to the optic nerve.

questions.question: What is a key symptom that differentiates arteritic from non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Temporal headache is a characteristic symptom of giant cell arteritis underlying arteritic AION, while non-arteritic AION usually presents without systemic symptoms.

questions.question: What laboratory finding supports the diagnosis of arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is markedly elevated in giant cell arteritis, which is the cause of arteritic AION, helping to differentiate it from the non-arteritic form.

questions.question: What visual complication is most common in untreated arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Without urgent treatment with corticosteroids, arteritic AION can progress to bilateral visual loss in a high percentage of cases, due to severe ischemia of the optic nerve.

questions.question: Which imaging study is a priority if arteritic AION is suspected?
questions.explanation: Fluorescein angiography is a priority to evaluate retinal and choroidal perfusion, showing filling defects in arteritic AION, although temporal artery biopsy confirms the diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of non-arteritic AION in terms of its treatment?
questions.explanation: Non-arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy lacks a treatment that has been shown to consistently modify visual prognosis, which is why it is currently considered without effective treatment.

questions.question: In non-arteritic AION, what therapeutic approach is currently recommended?
questions.explanation: As there is no effective treatment for non-arteritic AION, management focuses on controlling vascular risk factors such as hypertension, diabetes and dyslipidemia to prevent recurrences or involvement of the other eye.

questions.question: What typical finding is associated with non-arteritic AION on fundus examination?
questions.explanation: Non-arteritic AION is characterized by optic disc edema, generally sectoral or diffuse, which is the most common funduscopic sign in the acute phase, without another inflammatory or compressive cause.

questions.question: What is the expected visual evolution in the majority of patients with non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: In non-arteritic AION, visual acuity usually stabilizes after the acute phase without significant improvement, although it rarely progresses to total blindness; residual field defect is the norm.

questions.question: What risk factor is most frequently associated with non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: High blood pressure is the risk factor most commonly associated with non-arteritic AION, along with diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia and obstructive sleep apnea, all related to vascular compromise.

questions.question: What imaging study helps rule out secondary causes in suspected non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Orbital MRI with contrast allows us to rule out compressive or inflammatory lesions that may simulate AION, especially when papilledema is atypical or bilateral.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of ischemic optic neuropathy associated with nocturnal arterial hypertension?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal arterial hypertension alters vascular autoregulation, causing hypoperfusion of the optic nerve during the night, which triggers optic disc ischemia.

questions.question: Which cardiovascular risk factor is specifically associated with non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal arterial hypertension is a key risk factor for non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, since the lack of nocturnal decrease in blood pressure compromises optic nerve perfusion.

questions.question: What is the typical clinical presentation of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy manifests as painless, sudden, unilateral visual loss, often discovered upon awakening, related to nocturnal hypoperfusion.

questions.question: What imaging study is first-line to confirm the diagnosis of ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) allows evaluating the thickness of the retinal nerve fiber layer and detecting optic disc edema, being a key diagnostic tool in ischemic optic neuropathy.

questions.question: Which fundus finding is characteristic of nonarteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Optic disc edema is the classic ophthalmoscopic finding in the acute phase of non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, due to vascular congestion secondary to ischemia.

questions.question: What is the recommended initial management in a patient with non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy and nocturnal arterial hypertension?
questions.explanation: Strict control of blood pressure, especially nocturnal blood pressure, is essential to prevent the progression and recurrence of ischemic optic neuropathy, since hypoperfusion is the underlying mechanism.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal hypotension reduces optic nerve perfusion, especially in susceptible optic nerve heads, triggering ischemic neuropathy.

questions.question: Which drug should be avoided in patients with non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers may exacerbate nocturnal hypotension, further reducing optic nerve perfusion and worsening the risk of AION.

questions.question: What is the typical clinical presentation of non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Non-arteritic AION is characterized by sudden, painless, unilateral visual loss, often upon awakening, related to nocturnal hypotension.

questions.question: What funduscopic finding is characteristic of non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Sectoral or diffuse papilledema is the classic fundus finding during the acute phase of non-arteritic AION.

questions.question: What systemic risk factor is most frequently associated with non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: High blood pressure is the most common risk factor, along with diabetes and atherosclerosis, contributing to the susceptibility of the optic nerve to nocturnal hypotension.

questions.question: What preventive measure is recommended to avoid episodes of non-arteritic AION?
questions.explanation: Avoiding nocturnal hypotension, adjusting antihypertensive drugs and avoiding beta blockers, is key to preventing the recurrence of non-arteritic AION.

questions.question: What is the main clinical feature that differentiates anterior ischemic optic neuropathy from typical optic neuritis?
questions.explanation: Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy typically presents with sudden, painless visual loss, while optic neuritis is often associated with ocular pain, especially on movement.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023

questions.question: Which fundus finding is more suggestive of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy than of optic neuritis?
questions.explanation: Sectoral or diffuse papilledema is a characteristic finding in the acute phase of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, while in optic neuritis the disc is usually normal or with mild edema.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023

questions.question: Which imaging study is most useful in differentiating ischemic optic neuropathy from optic neuritis?
questions.explanation: Brain MRI with contrast is key, since in optic neuritis it usually shows enhancement of the optic nerve, while in ischemic optic neuropathy there is no pathological enhancement.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023

questions.question: Which visual field alteration is most characteristic of anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: The altitudinal defect, especially lower, is the most classic visual field pattern in anterior ischemic optic neuropathy, unlike the central scotoma typical of optic neuritis.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023

questions.question: What systemic risk factor is most frequently associated with arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: Arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy is associated with giant cell arteritis, which frequently manifests with polymyalgia rheumatica, temporary headache, and elevation of acute phase reactants.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy?
questions.explanation: In the arteritic form (associated with giant cell arteritis), the treatment of choice is intravenous corticosteroids in high doses to prevent visual loss in the contralateral eye and systemic complications.
hashtags: Basic and Clinical Science Course (BCSC) AAO 2023
